title: Feeling too much heat in the body
excerpt: Feeling too much heat in the body, with burning sensations, acid reflux, or a short temper, is something Ayurveda has understood for centuries. It calls this state "pitta vriddhi," and the classics offer a gentle, food-first path back to coolness.
at_a_glance: Pitta vriddhi, excess of the fire principle, causes burning, acidity, and irritability in the body and mind.
Cool, sweet, and bitter foods and drinks reduce pitta; spicy, sour, and salty foods and irregular mealtimes increase it.
Consistent sleep before 11 p.m., morning exercise, and midday rest are as important as diet for managing body heat.

body.heading: that familiar burning feeling
paragraphs: You know the feeling: lunch sits like a coal in your stomach, your skin flushes in the afternoon, you snap at someone over nothing, and by evening there is a dull burning behind the sternum. Many men brush this off as stress or spicy food. Ayurveda sees it differently. These are signs that pitta, the dosha of fire and transformation, has accumulated beyond its healthy measure.

Pitta is not bad in itself. The Caraka Samhita teaches that pitta governs digestion, vision, skin colour, courage, and the capacity for clear thought (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). In its balanced state it gives a man sharp intellect and a good appetite. When it increases, that same fire turns on the body. The classical term is pitta vriddhi, an excess or aggravation of the fire principle. The Ashtanga Hridaya lists its cardinal signs as daha (burning), pita varna (yellowish tinge to skin or urine), krodha (anger or irritability), and excessive thirst (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

body.heading: what stokes the fire
paragraphs: Understanding the cause is half the remedy. Pitta aggravates in summer and in the middle of the day, which is why many men feel worst between noon and two in the afternoon. But season and time of day are only part of the story. The Caraka Samhita identifies foods and behaviours that directly fan pitta: pungent, sour, and salty tastes in excess; alcohol; excessive exposure to heat or sun; skipping meals or eating at irregular times; and prolonged anger or competitive striving (Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana).

The modern Indian man's life stacks several of these together. A spicy breakfast, a missed meal, a commute in the blazing midday sun, and a high-pressure meeting can each add a little heat. Over days and weeks, the accumulation crosses a threshold and symptoms appear. Bhavaprakasha, a later classical text that remains widely consulted, echoes this understanding and adds that the pitta seat is primarily the small intestine; disturbance there rises upward and outward (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda).

body.heading: cooling from the inside out
paragraphs: The Ayurvedic approach is not dramatic. It works through the principle of opposite qualities: what is hot is balanced by what is cool, what is sharp is softened by what is mild. The Ashtanga Hridaya's chapter on seasonal regimens recommends sweet, bitter, and astringent tastes for reducing pitta, along with cool (not ice-cold) foods and a slower, less competitive pace of life (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

In practice this translates into a few concrete dietary choices. Ripe sweet fruit like pomegranate, coconut water, and soaked raisins are classical pitta pacifiers. Cow's ghee in small amounts, buttermilk lightly spiced with coriander and cumin rather than chillies, and rice with dal are gentle on the digestive fire without stoking it further. Reduce or remove very sour pickles, vinegar, fermented drinks, and red chillies, especially at midday when pitta is already at its peak.

Bhavaprakasha gives prominence to coriander as a plant that cools the body and supports digestion simultaneously (Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu). A simple drink of fresh coriander water, made by soaking a teaspoon of whole coriander seeds overnight in a glass of water and straining it in the morning, is an old home remedy that aligns well with this classical view. Fennel seed water after meals serves a similar purpose and also reduces the bloating that often accompanies excess pitta.

body.heading: daily habits that hold the heat down
paragraphs: Food is important, but pitta also responds strongly to routine and rest. Going to bed before 11 p.m. matters, because the pitta period of the night runs from 10 p.m. to 2 a.m. and staying awake through it causes mental pitta to rise, contributing to a racing mind and irritability the next day. Eating three meals at roughly fixed times prevents the digestive fire from burning the stomach lining when empty.

Exercise is best done in the cool of the morning, not at midday. Competitive sports in harsh sun, hot yoga in summer, or intense gym sessions at noon all push pitta higher. A walk in the early morning, a swim, or gentle yoga asanas with an emphasis on forward folds and supported poses help channel pitta energy without inflaming it. The Caraka Samhita recommends avoiding excessive physical and mental exertion as a core pitta management strategy (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

Rest is not laziness. Taking ten minutes after lunch before returning to work, sitting in shade rather than direct sun, and learning to step away from an argument before the anger compounds all reduce pitta in ways that no herb can fully replace.

body.heading: when to see a doctor or vaidya
paragraphs: The habits described here are general and supportive. Please see a qualified doctor or Ayurvedic vaidya if you experience any of the following: persistent burning pain in the chest or abdomen, blood in vomit or stool, unexplained weight loss, yellowing of the skin or eyes, fever that does not settle within a couple of days, or burning during urination. These signs may point to conditions, such as peptic ulcer disease, liver problems, or urinary tract infection, that need medical diagnosis and treatment.
This article is general information written for a curious reader. It is not a diagnosis and it does not replace personalised advice from a physician or trained Ayurvedic practitioner.

try_this_today.title: Coriander seed water in the morning
try_this_today.description: Soak one teaspoon of whole coriander seeds in a glass of water overnight. Strain and drink on an empty stomach. Bhavaprakasha praises coriander as a cooling digestive herb.

try_this_today.title: Fennel water after meals
try_this_today.description: Steep half a teaspoon of fennel seeds in a cup of warm (not hot) water for five minutes, strain, and sip after lunch and dinner. It reduces bloating and settles the post-meal burning that often accompanies excess pitta.

try_this_today.title: Switch chilli for coriander and cumin in your dal
try_this_today.description: Reduce the red or green chilli in your everyday dal and tarka. Season instead with cumin, a little coriander powder, and a pinch of turmeric. The flavour remains satisfying and the heat load drops considerably.

try_this_today.title: Coconut water mid-afternoon
try_this_today.description: Replace a chai or coffee break around 3 p.m. with fresh or packaged coconut water with no added sugar. This is one of the simplest pitta-cooling drinks available year-round in India.

try_this_today.title: Bed before 11 p.m.
try_this_today.description: Pitta governs the 10 p.m. to 2 a.m. window. Staying awake through it tends to produce a second wind of mental alertness that turns into racing thoughts and irritability the next morning. An earlier bedtime is a low-effort, high-impact change.

try_this_today.title: Move exercise to early morning
try_this_today.description: If you currently exercise at midday or in the late afternoon sun, shift your workout to before 8 a.m. Morning exercise channels pitta energy constructively without overheating the body during the pitta-dominant midday hours.

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: Praised in Bhavaprakasha Nighantu as a cooling herb that supports digestion without adding heat. Used as a seed water or powder in everyday cooking.

ingredients.name: Fennel
ingredients.description: A gentle digestive and cooling herb; fennel water after meals reduces bloating and the burning sensation associated with excess pitta.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: Balances digestive fire without aggravating pitta when used in place of sharp chilies in everyday dal and sabzi.

words_to_know.definition: One of the three doshas; governs heat, digestion, and transformation in the body and mind.
Aggravation or excess of pitta beyond its healthy level; the root of body heat symptoms.
Burning sensation, one of the key signs listed in the classics for pitta excess.
Anger or irritability; considered both a cause and a symptom of excess pitta.
One of three fundamental physiological and psychological principles in Ayurveda: vata, pitta, and kapha.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana (chapters on the qualities and functions of doshas, daily regimen, and physical exertion) · Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana (causes of dosha aggravation, including dietary and behavioural factors) · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (signs of pitta vriddhi and seasonal dietary regimens) · Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda (dosha sthana: pitta's primary seat in the region between the two intestines and its role in systemic heat) · Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu (properties of dhanyaka / coriander as a cooling digestive herb)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
